questions.stem: What does the abbreviation BDD stand for in this lecture?
questions.explanation: BDD stands for Behaviour-Driven Development: behaviour described in a common domain language, defined collaboratively, executed like tests, forming living documentation. 'Behaviour-Driven Design', 'Build-Driven Development' and 'Boundary-Defined Design' are invented expansions not used by the deck. (BDD p.4)

questions.stem: Which item is listed among the 'typical test issues' that motivate BDD?

options.text: Many technical and often irrelevant details clutter the test
Tests run too quickly to be observed
Tests are too readable by non-developers
Tests produce too much living documentation

questions.explanation: The deck's list of typical test issues includes 'many technical and often irrelevant details'. The other options invert real problems: tests being too readable or producing too much documentation are not complaints — they are exactly what BDD aims to achieve. (BDD p.3)

questions.stem: According to the deck, ordinary tests suffer because they can be read by whom?
questions.explanation: A motivating test issue is that conventional tests 'can only be read by developers'. BDD's fix is domain-language scenarios readable by experts too. The claim is not that only experts or only tools can read them, nor that everyone reads them equally — that equal readability is the goal BDD strives for. (BDD p.3)

questions.stem: Which is cited as a typical problem with conventional tests that BDD seeks to fix?

options.text: The point of the test is often hard to grasp
The test always documents the domain perfectly
The test is always free of duplication
The test is automatically readable by experts

questions.explanation: The deck lists 'the point of the test is often hard to grasp' as a typical issue. The other three describe ideal qualities BDD adds, not pre-existing problems — conventional tests are criticised precisely for duplication and unreadability by experts. (BDD p.3)

questions.stem: Conventional tests are criticised by the deck for which maintainability problem?

options.text: Being too short to be useful
Running only on the developer's machine

questions.explanation: Code duplication across tests is one of the listed typical test issues. Excessive domain language is what BDD adds (a benefit, not a flaw); 'too short' and 'only on the developer's machine' are not among the deck's cited issues. (BDD p.3)

questions.stem: Why does the deck argue that the same BDD artefact can serve developers, testers, and domain experts simultaneously?

options.text: It runs (developer), is the acceptance criterion (tester), and is readable documentation (domain expert)
It is compiled to three separate binaries, one per audience
It is translated automatically into three natural languages
It is stored in three different repositories for three teams

questions.explanation: BDD raises behaviour to domain language so one artefact runs (for developers), acts as the acceptance criterion (for testers) and reads as documentation (for domain experts). The other options describe mechanical duplication, not the single-artefact-three-audiences idea the deck stresses. (BDD p.4)

questions.stem: In the JGiven pancake test, the step methods are written in which naming style?
questions.explanation: JGiven step methods use snake_case (e.g. an_egg(), the_cook_fries_the_dough_in_a_pan()) so the Java reads like the prose scenario. camelCase, PascalCase and kebab-case would not reproduce the readable sentence form. (BDD p.10)

questions.stem: Trace the pancake state pipeline. Which sequence correctly describes the @...ScenarioState flow?

options.text: Given provides ingredients; When consumes ingredients and provides meal; Then consumes meal
Given consumes meal; When provides ingredients; Then provides dough
Then provides ingredients; Given consumes meal; When consumes dough
All three stages only use @ScenarioState bidirectionally

questions.explanation: The pipeline is: Given provides ingredients, When consumes ingredients (+Cook) and provides meal/dough, Then consumes meal and asserts. The other orderings reverse producer/consumer roles, and the deck uses directional @Provided/@Expected, not only bidirectional @ScenarioState. (BDD p.14-16)

questions.stem: A developer annotates the Then stage's meal field @ProvidedScenarioState instead of @ExpectedScenarioState. What is the most likely consequence?

options.text: The Then stage will not receive the meal value produced upstream, so its assertion lacks the produced data
The scenario compiles into a Gherkin file automatically
The Given stage stops producing ingredients
The HTML report switches to German automatically

questions.explanation: @ProvidedScenarioState marks the field as something this stage outputs, so the Then stage would not be wired to consume the upstream meal — the assertion loses the produced value. The other options are unrelated effects (Gherkin generation, Given behaviour, report language). (BDD p.13, p.16)

questions.stem: How do the JGiven console and HTML5 reports satisfy the test-issue 'tests cannot be used as documentation'?

options.text: The executed scenario reads identically to the spec, so it doubles as living documentation
They hide all scenario text so nothing can be misread
They export the source code as a PDF manual
They replace tests with prose written by hand

questions.explanation: Because the executed scenario reads like the specification, the report is itself readable documentation that stays in sync — addressing the 'unusable as documentation' issue. They do not hide text, export source PDFs, or replace tests with hand prose. (BDD p.3-4, p.17-18)

questions.stem: What did the TNG report say happened to readability and reusability of test code with JGiven?
questions.explanation: TNG reports readability and reusability greatly improved. The deck does not claim they worsened, stayed identical, or were unmeasurable. (BDD p.19)

questions.stem: What caveat accompanies TNG's claim that maintenance costs of automated tests were reduced?

options.text: There were no hard numbers to quantify the reduction
The reduction only applied to GUI tests
Maintenance costs actually rose for new developers

questions.explanation: TNG reports reduced maintenance costs but with 'no hard numbers' — a qualitative claim. It was not limited to GUI tests, did not raise costs for newcomers (it was easy to learn), and did not require dropping JUnit. (BDD p.19)

questions.stem: The JGiven summary slide highlights easy integration with which existing test infrastructures?

options.text: Maven and Gradle as test runners

questions.explanation: JGiven integrates easily with JUnit and TestNG. Cucumber/JBehave are competing BDD frameworks, Selenium/Cypress are web UI tools, and Maven/Gradle are build tools (JGiven has Maven and Jenkins plugins, but the test infrastructures named are JUnit/TestNG). (BDD p.20)

questions.stem: JGiven 'buys low maintenance and reuse by living in Java, but the price is...'. What completes the deck's central trade-off?

options.text: ...non-developers can read the reports yet cannot author the scenarios
...the tests can no longer run on CI servers
...the reports are no longer readable by anyone

questions.explanation: Living in Java lowers maintenance and boosts reuse, but at the cost that non-developers can read reports yet not author scenarios. CI still works, reports stay readable, and JGiven remains open-source Apache 2 — so the other completions are false. (BDD p.20)

questions.stem: What does AssertJ-Swing embed in HTML reports when a GUI test fails?

options.text: Screenshots of the failed GUI test
The full source code of the app
A video recording of the whole session
A memory dump of the JVM

questions.explanation: AssertJ-Swing can embed screenshots of failed GUI tests in HTML reports. It does not embed full source, video recordings, or JVM memory dumps. (BDD p.29)

questions.stem: Which test frameworks can AssertJ-Swing be used with?
questions.explanation: AssertJ-Swing works with TestNG or JUnit. It is not limited to Cucumber/JBehave or Spock, and it is not a standalone-only runner. (BDD p.29)

questions.stem: The BDD deck itself — does it mention JHotDraw or walk the eight-phase change process?

options.text: No; the JHotDraw and eight-phase links are course-frame synthesis, not deck claims
Yes; the deck dedicates a slide to JHotDraw figures
Yes; the deck names all eight phases explicitly
Yes; JHotDraw is the deck's running example instead of pancakes

questions.explanation: The deck does NOT mention JHotDraw or name the eight phases; those connections are course-frame synthesis. The deck's running example is the pancake scenario, not JHotDraw. (guide gotchas; BDD p.5)

questions.stem: Why is AssertJ-Swing the relevant BDD tool for verifying a JHotDraw change at the GUI level?

options.text: JHotDraw is a Swing drawing editor, and AssertJ-Swing automates Swing GUI tests
JHotDraw is a web app, and AssertJ-Swing drives browsers
JHotDraw is a database, and AssertJ-Swing runs SQL
JHotDraw is a CLI tool needing no GUI testing

questions.explanation: JHotDraw is a Java Swing drawing editor, so AssertJ-Swing (which automates Swing GUI tests) is the natural verification tool. JHotDraw is not a web app, database, or CLI tool. (BDD p.29; course frame)

questions.stem: For a JHotDraw scenario 'Given a drawing with one rectangle, When the user drags it 50px right, Then x increases by 50', which tool plays which role?

options.text: JGiven implements the scenario in Java; AssertJ-Swing drives the drag; AssertJ asserts the new coordinate
Cucumber implements it; Hamcrest drives the GUI; Fest asserts
AssertJ-Swing writes the scenario; JGiven drives the GUI; JUnit asserts only
The scenario cannot be automated because JHotDraw is Swing

questions.explanation: JGiven expresses the scenario in Java, AssertJ-Swing simulates the drag, and AssertJ asserts the coordinate. The Cucumber/Hamcrest/Fest combo is not the course toolchain, the roles in option C are scrambled, and Swing GUIs are automatable (that is AssertJ-Swing's purpose). (BDD p.10-16, p.29; course frame)

questions.stem: How does JGiven's stage reuse pay off on a large case study like JHotDraw?

options.text: A stage such as GivenADrawing can be reused across many figure scenarios
Each scenario must rewrite all stages from scratch
Stages can only be used once and then discarded
Stages must be authored by domain experts

questions.explanation: Because stages are ordinary reusable classes, a GivenADrawing stage is reused across many JHotDraw figure scenarios. Stages are not rewritten per scenario, not single-use, and authored by developers, not experts. (BDD p.11; course frame)

questions.stem: JHotDraw is built on GoF patterns (Composite, Observer, Strategy). How do BDD scenarios relate to these patterns?

options.text: They verify the behaviour the patterns produce (e.g. an Observer refreshing the view) without coupling to the pattern's internal structure
They replace the patterns with simpler procedural code
They assert directly on each pattern's private fields
They forbid the use of design patterns in tested code

questions.explanation: BDD verifies observable behaviour (e.g. the Observer refreshing the view) without coupling to internal structure — behaviour over implementation. It does not replace patterns, assert on private fields, or forbid patterns. (course frame; [GHJV94])

questions.stem: Which is a genuine 'typical test issue' the deck lists (choose the real one)?

options.text: Tests cannot be used as documentation
Tests are too readable by experts
Tests have too few technical details
Tests run on too many machines

questions.explanation: 'Tests cannot be used as documentation' is one of the listed issues. The others invert reality: too-readable, too-few-details and too-many-machines are not the deck's complaints (it complains of irrelevant detail and developer-only readability). (BDD p.3)

questions.stem: In the deck's Condition example, which two AssertJ calls test the divisibility predicate?
questions.explanation: The example asserts assertThat(12).is(evenDivBySix) and assertThat(8).isNot(evenDivBySix). contains/startsWith are String/array methods, given/then are JGiven, and matches/filter are not the application calls. (BDD p.27)

questions.stem: You role-play verifying a JHotDraw change: 'Given a drawing with one rectangle, When the user drags it 50px right, Then x increases by 50.' Which tool drives the actual drag on the Swing canvas?
questions.explanation: Driving real GUI interaction (drag 'n drop) on a Swing app like JHotDraw is AssertJ-Swing's job. JUnit/Hamcrest only assert values; Cucumber parses plain text, it does not drive a GUI. (BDD p.29)

questions.stem: A snippet reads: 'assertThat(actual).contains("is").startsWith("This");'. Which library is this?
questions.explanation: Chained fluent assertions like contains().startsWith() are AssertJ's signature style. Hamcrest uses assertThat(x, matcher), JUnit uses assertEquals, and Mockito mocks. (BDD p.25)

questions.stem: A snippet defines 'new Condition<Integer>(){ public boolean matches(Integer v){ return v%6==0; } }' and uses assertThat(12).is(...). Which AssertJ feature is shown?
questions.explanation: Overriding matches() and applying it with is()/isNot() is the custom Condition functor. A custom assertion subclasses AbstractAssert; this snippet does neither that nor Swing lookup. (BDD p.27)

questions.stem: A class extends 'Stage<GivenIngredients>'. What does the generic parameter (the self-type) enable?
questions.explanation: Stage<SELF> is the self-type that makes 'return this' return the concrete stage type for fluent chaining. It is unrelated to DI, reports, or threading. (BDD p.11,14)

questions.stem: A test imports 'static org.assertj.core.api.Assertions.assertThat;'. Which library and purpose does this indicate?

options.text: AssertJ — to use the fluent assertThat() factory in Then steps

questions.explanation: The org.assertj.core.api.Assertions.assertThat static import is AssertJ's fluent factory, used in Then steps. It is not Hamcrest, JUnit, or JGiven's stage API. (BDD p.16,24)

questions.stem: Because JHotDraw is a Swing application prone to threading mistakes, why is AssertJ-Swing's EDT support valuable when verifying a change?

options.text: It removes the need for any assertions
It can test violations of Swing's Event Dispatch Thread rules

questions.explanation: AssertJ-Swing can detect EDT (threading-rule) violations, important for a GUI framework like JHotDraw. It does not compile, remove assertions, or port to JavaFX. (BDD p.29)

questions.stem: Because JGiven ships Maven and Jenkins plugins, what does this enable for the change process?

options.text: Scenarios run automatically in CI, tying Verification to the Conclusion/CI phase

questions.explanation: Maven/Jenkins plugins let scenarios execute automatically on every integration, connecting Verification to CI. They do not force manual runs or change authoring/readability. (BDD p.20)

questions.stem: You need to assert that a String both contains a substring and starts with a prefix in one readable statement. Which is most appropriate?

options.text: A Hamcrest assertThat with a single matcher

questions.explanation: AssertJ's fluent chain reads as one sentence and is the deck's recommended style. Separate assertTrue calls are less readable, and Mockito is for mocks, not assertions. (BDD p.25)

questions.stem: You want a reusable predicate 'is evenly divisible by six' applied to many integer assertions. Which AssertJ feature fits best?

options.text: A custom AbstractAssert subclass per number
A custom Condition used with is()/isNot()

questions.explanation: A reusable predicate is exactly a Condition functor (override matches, apply via is/isNot). A full AbstractAssert subclass is heavier; Swing and Gherkin are irrelevant here. (BDD p.27)

questions.stem: Your domain type Student needs natural assertions like isInMiddleSchool() and isSenior(). Which approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Define a single Condition and reuse it for both checks
Subclass AbstractAssert (StudentAssert) plus Assertions for the factory

questions.explanation: Multiple named domain assertions belong on a custom AbstractAssert subclass with a matching Assertions factory. A lone Condition gives is()/isNot(), not named methods. (BDD p.28)

questions.stem: To verify that dragging a figure on JHotDraw's canvas updates its position, which tool is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Driving and verifying real Swing GUI interaction is AssertJ-Swing's purpose. Plain assertions cannot perform the drag; Cucumber parses text, not GUIs. (BDD p.29)

questions.stem: You need a single assertion library that is maintained and replaces JUnit/Hamcrest/Fest. Which does the deck recommend?
questions.explanation: AssertJ is the deck's recommendation: maintained, a near-superset, and readable. Hamcrest is stagnant, Fest abandonware, JUnit underpowered. (BDD p.23)

questions.stem: You want the factory call that returns the correct assertion type for whatever value you pass. Which is the right entry point?
questions.explanation: assertThat() is AssertJ's type-specific factory returning the right assert per type. AbstractAssert is a base class you subclass, matches() is a Condition method, and assertEquals is JUnit. (BDD p.24)

questions.stem: A study slide draft says 'pick Hamcrest for Then-step assertions.' Per the deck, what single word best describes Hamcrest's state?
questions.explanation: The deck calls Hamcrest stagnant (and ugly) and steers you to AssertJ instead. It is not cutting-edge, JDK-bundled, or the deck's recommendation. (BDD p.22-23)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'The BDD deck cites JHotDraw and walks the eight-phase change process.' What is accurate?

options.text: The deck does NOT mention JHotDraw or the eight phases; that link is course-frame synthesis
The deck names all eight phases
The deck devotes a slide to JHotDraw
The deck replaces JHotDraw with Swing examples on every slide

questions.explanation: The BDD deck is tool-focused and does not name JHotDraw or the eight phases; the connection is supplied by the course frame, not a deck slide. (BDD p.1)

questions.stem: How does BDD connect the Verification phase to the Conclusion/CI phase in the change process?

options.text: Verification must finish before any code is written
JGiven's Maven/Jenkins plugins run the scenarios automatically on every integration
CI replaces the need for scenarios

questions.explanation: JGiven's CI plugins execute scenarios automatically at integration, linking Verification to Conclusion/CI. CI does not replace scenarios, and the phase order is not reversed. (BDD p.20)

questions.stem: On a sprawling case study like JHotDraw, what about JGiven stage reuse makes it especially worthwhile?

options.text: A stage like GivenADrawing can be reused across many figure scenarios
Large apps cannot use stages at all

questions.explanation: On a big app, a reusable GivenADrawing stage serves many scenarios, amplifying JGiven's modularity benefit. Stages do not require .feature files or disable AssertJ. (BDD p.11)

questions.stem: Which Swing-specific risk in JHotDraw can AssertJ-Swing help verify?

options.text: Violations of the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) threading rules

questions.explanation: AssertJ-Swing can test EDT threading-rule violations, relevant to a Swing GUI like JHotDraw. Heap, network and DB concerns are outside its scope. (BDD p.29)

questions.stem: Interpret 'assertThat(actualArray).contains("is").startsWith("This");' where actualArray is a String[]. What does it demonstrate?

options.text: Polymorphism — the same methods work on String and String[] via type-specific asserts
That contains() is a JUnit method
A compile error because arrays lack these methods

questions.explanation: The same contains()/startsWith() names work on both String and String[] because assertThat returns the right type-specific assert — polymorphism. It compiles and is AssertJ, not JUnit. (BDD p.25)

questions.stem: Interpret the Condition example: 'assertThat(12).is(evenDivBySix); assertThat(8).isNot(evenDivBySix);'. What is being checked?

options.text: 12 satisfies the divisible-by-six predicate; 8 does not

questions.explanation: is(condition) checks 12 % 6 == 0 (true), isNot(condition) checks 8 is not divisible by six (true). It is not an equality or array-contains check. (BDD p.27)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'AssertJ contains() and startsWith() are different APIs for String and array.' Correct it.

options.text: They are the same polymorphic methods; assertThat returns the right type-specific assert
They are different APIs requiring separate imports

questions.explanation: The same method names work on String and array via the type-specific assert returned by assertThat — polymorphism, not separate APIs. Arrays do support them. (BDD p.25)

questions.stem: Applied/cause: if a JHotDraw GUI test mutates a figure off the EDT, what can AssertJ-Swing reveal?
questions.explanation: AssertJ-Swing can surface EDT threading-rule violations. Leaks, SQL injection and merge conflicts are unrelated to its checks. (BDD p.29)

questions.stem: AssertJ is primarily used inside which BDD scenario clause?
questions.explanation: AssertJ performs assertions in the Then clause (with sanity checks possible in When). It is not Given-, When-only, or And-only. (BDD p.16,24)

questions.stem: Which AssertJ method asserts exact equality in the pancake Then step?
questions.explanation: ThenMeal uses assertThat(meal).isEqualTo("pancake"). contains/startsWith are substring/prefix checks and is() applies a Condition. (BDD p.16)

questions.stem: Which library provides the fluent assertThat used in Then steps?
questions.explanation: AssertJ provides the fluent assertThat factory for Then steps. Mockito mocks, Log4j logs, and assertEquals is JUnit's non-fluent assert. (BDD p.24)

questions.stem: AssertJ-Swing is specifically for testing what kind of application?
questions.explanation: AssertJ-Swing automates Swing GUI tests. CLI jobs, REST services and DB procedures are outside its scope. (BDD p.29)

questions.stem: Which AssertJ feature is a reusable predicate applied via is()/isNot()?
questions.explanation: A Condition is the reusable predicate used with is()/isNot(). A custom assertion adds named methods; assertThat is the factory; Swing is GUI testing. (BDD p.27)
